scene_title: Before Gloucester's castle; Kent in the stocks

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: ’Tis strange that they should so depart from home,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And not send back my messenger.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: As I learn’d,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The night before there was no purpose in them

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Of this remove.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Hail to thee, noble master!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Ha!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Makest thou this shame thy pastime?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: No, my lord.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Ha, ha! he wears cruel garters. Horses are tied by the heads,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: dogs and bears by the neck, monkeys by the loins, and men

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: by the legs: when a man’s over-lusty at legs, then he wears

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: wooden nether-stocks.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What’s he that hath so much thy place mistook

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To set thee here?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: It is both he and she;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Your son and daughter.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Yes.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, I say.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I say, yea.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, no, they would not.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Yes, they have.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: By Jupiter, I swear, no.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: By Juno, I swear, ay.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: They durst not do ’t;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: They could not, would not do ’t; ’tis worse than murder,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To do upon respect such violent outrage:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Resolve me, with all modest haste, which way

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thou mightst deserve, or they impose, this usage,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Coming from us.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: My lord, when at their home

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I did commend your highness’ letters to them,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Ere I was risen from the place that show’d

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: My duty kneeling, came there a reeking post,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Stew’d in his haste, half breathless, panting forth

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: From Goneril his mistress salutations;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Deliver’d letters, spite of intermission,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Which presently they read: on whose contents,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: They summon’d up their meiny, straight took horse;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Commanded me to follow, and attend

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The leisure of their answer; gave me cold looks:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: And meeting here the other messenger,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Whose welcome, I perceived, had poison’d mine—

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Being the very fellow that of late

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Display’d so saucily against your highness—

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Having more man than wit about me, drew:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: He raised the house with loud and coward cries.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Your son and daughter found this trespass worth

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The shame which here it suffers.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Winter’s not gone yet, if the wild-geese fly that way.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Fathers that wear rags

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Do make their children blind;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: But fathers that bear bags

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Shall see their children kind.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Fortune, that arrant whore,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Ne’er turns the key to the poor.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: But, for all this, thou shalt have as many dolours

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: for thy daughters as thou canst tell in a year.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: O, how this mother swells up toward my heart!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Hysterica passio, down, thou climbing sorrow,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thy element’s below! Where is this daughter?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: With the earl, sir, here within.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Follow me not;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Stay here.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Made you no more offence but what you speak of?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: None.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: How chance the king comes with so small a train?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: And thou hadst been set i’ the stocks for that question, thou

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: hadst well deserved it.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Why, fool?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: We’ll set thee to school to an ant, to teach thee there’s

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: no labouring i’ the winter. All that follow their noses are led

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: by their eyes but blind men; and there’s not a nose among

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: twenty but can smell him that’s stinking. Let go thy hold when

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: a great wheel runs down a hill, lest it break thy neck

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: with following it: but the great one that goes up the hill,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: let him draw thee after. When a wise man gives thee better

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: counsel, give me mine again: I would have none but knaves follow

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: it, since a fool gives it.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Where learned you this, fool?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Not i’ the stocks, fool.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Deny to speak with me? They are sick? they are weary?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: They have travell’d all the night? Mere fetches;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The images of revolt and flying off.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Fetch me a better answer.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My dear lord,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: You know the fiery quality of the duke;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: How unremoveable and fix’d he is

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: In his own course.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Vengeance! plague! death! confusion!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Fiery? what quality? Why, Gloucester, Gloucester,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I’ld speak with the Duke of Cornwall and his wife.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Well, my good lord, I have inform’d them so.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Inform’d them! Dost thou understand me, man?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Ay, my good lord.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The king would speak with Cornwall; the dear father

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Would with his daughter speak, commands her service:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Are they inform’d of this? My breath and blood!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Fiery? the fiery duke? Tell the hot duke that—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, but not yet: may be he is not well:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Infirmity doth still neglect all office

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Whereto our health is bound; we are not ourselves

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: When nature, being oppress’d, commands the mind

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To suffer with the body: I’ll forbear;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And am fall’n out with my more headier will,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To take the indisposed and sickly fit

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: For the sound man. Death on my state! wherefore Looking on Kent.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Should he sit here? This act persuades me

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That this remotion of the duke and her

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Is practise only. Give me my servant forth.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Go tell the duke and ’s wife I’ld speak with them,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Now, presently: bid them come forth and hear me,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Or at their chamber-door I’ll beat the drum

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Till it cry sleep to death.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I would have all well betwixt you.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: O me, my heart, my rising heart! but, down!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Cry to it, nuncle, as the cockney did to the eels when

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: she put ’em i’ the paste alive; she knapped ’em o’ the

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: coxcombs with a stick, and cried ‘Down, wantons, down!’ ’Twas her brother

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: that, in pure kindness to his horse, buttered his hay.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Good morrow to you both.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Hail to your grace! Kent is set at liberty.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: I am glad to see your highness.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Regan, I think you are; I know what reason

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I have to think so: if thou shouldst not be glad,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I would divorce me from thy mother’s tomb,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Sepulchring an adultress.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To Kent. O, are you free?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Some other time for that. Beloved Regan,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thy sister’s naught: O Regan, she hath tied

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Sharp-tooth’d unkindness, like a vulture, here:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I can scarce speak to thee; thou’lt not believe

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: With how depraved a quality—O Regan!

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: I pray you, sir, take patience: I have hope.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: You less know how to value her desert

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Than she to scant her duty.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Say, how is that?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: I cannot think my sister in the least

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Would fail her obligation: if, sir, perchance

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: She have restrain’d the riots of your followers,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: ’Tis on such ground, and to such wholesome end,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: As clears her from all blame.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: My curses on her!

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: O, sir, you are old.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Nature in you stands on the very verge

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Of her confine: you should be ruled and led

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: By some discretion, that discerns your state

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Better than you yourself. Therefore, I pray you,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: That to our sister you do make return;

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Say you have wrong’d her, sir.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Ask her forgiveness?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Do you but mark how this becomes the house:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: “Dear daughter, I confess that I am old;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Age is unnecessary: on my knees I beg

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That you’ll vouchsafe me raiment, bed, and food.”

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Good sir, no more; these are unsightly tricks:

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Return you to my sister.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Never, Regan:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: She hath abated me of half my train;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Look’d black upon me; struck me with her tongue,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Most serpent-like, upon the very heart:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: All the stored vengeances of heaven fall

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: On her ingrateful top! Strike her young bones,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You taking airs, with lameness!

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Fie, sir, fie!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You nimble lightnings, dart your blinding flames

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Into her scornful eyes! Infect her beauty,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You fen-suck’d fogs, drawn by the powerful sun,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To fall and blast her pride!

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: O the blest gods! so will you wish on me,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: When the rash mood is on.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, Regan, thou shalt never have my curse:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thy tender-hefted nature shall not give

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thee o’er to harshness: her eyes are fierce; but thine

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Do comfort and not burn. ’Tis not in thee

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To grudge my pleasures, to cut off my train,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To bandy hasty words, to scant my sizes,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And in conclusion to oppose the bolt

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Against my coming in: thou better know’st

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The offices of nature, bond of childhood,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Effects of courtesy, dues of gratitude;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thy half o’ the kingdom hast thou not forgot,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Wherein I thee endow’d.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Good sir, to the purpose.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Who put my man i’ the stocks? Tucket within.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: What trumpet’s that?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: I know’t, my sister’s: this approves her letter,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: That she would soon be here.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: This is a slave, whose easy-borrow’d pride

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Dwells in the fickle grace of her he follows.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Out, varlet, from my sight!

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: What means your grace?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Who stock’d my servant? Regan, I have good hope

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thou didst not know on’t. Who comes here? O heavens,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Why not by the hand, sir? How have I offended?

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: All’s not offence that indiscretion finds

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: And dotage terms so.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: O sides, you are too tough;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Will you yet hold? How came my man i’ the stocks?

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: I set him there, sir: but his own disorders

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Deserved much less advancement.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You! did you?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: I pray you, father, being weak, seem so.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: If, till the expiration of your month,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: You will return and sojourn with my sister,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Dismissing half your train, come then to me:

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: I am now from home, and out of that provision

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Which shall be needful for your entertainment.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Return to her, and fifty men dismiss’d?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, rather I abjure all roofs, and choose

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To wage against the enmity o’ the air;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To be a comrade with the wolf and owl—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Necessity’s sharp pinch! Return with her?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why, the hot-blooded France, that dowerless took

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Our youngest born, I could as well be brought

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To knee his throne, and, squire-like; pension beg

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To keep base life afoot. Return with her?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Persuade me rather to be slave and sumpter

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To this detested groom.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: At your choice, sir.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I prithee, daughter, do not make me mad:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I will not trouble thee, my child; farewell:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: We’ll no more meet, no more see one another:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: But yet thou art my flesh, my blood, my daughter;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Or rather a disease that’s in my flesh,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Which I must needs call mine: thou art a boil,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: A plague-sore, an embossed carbuncle,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: In my corrupted blood. But I’ll not chide thee;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Let shame come when it will, I do not call it:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I do not bid the thunder-bearer shoot,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Nor tell tales of thee to high-judging Jove:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Mend when thou canst; be better at thy leisure:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I can be patient; I can stay with Regan,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I and my hundred knights.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Not altogether so:

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: I look’d not for you yet, nor am provided

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: For your fit welcome. Give ear, sir, to my sister;

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: For those that mingle reason with your passion

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Must be content to think you old, and so—

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: But she knows what she does.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Is this well spoken?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: I dare avouch it, sir: what, fifty followers?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Is it not well? What should you need of more?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Yea, or so many, sith that both charge and danger

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Speak ’gainst so great a number? How, in one house,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Should many people, under two commands,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Hold amity? ’Tis hard; almost impossible.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Why might not you, my lord, receive attendance

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: From those that she calls servants or from mine?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Why not, my lord? If then they chanced to slack you,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: We could control them. If you will come to me—

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: For now I spy a danger—I entreat you

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: To bring but five and twenty: to no more

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Will I give place or notice.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I gave you all—

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: And in good time you gave it.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Made you my guardians, my depositaries;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: But kept a reservation to be follow’d

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: With such a number. What, must I come to you

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: With five and twenty, Regan? said you so?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: And speak’t again, my lord; no more with me.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Those wicked creatures yet do look well-favour’d,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: When others are more wicked: not being the worst

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Stands in some rank of praise.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To Goneril. I’ll go with thee:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thy fifty yet doth double five and twenty,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And thou art twice her love.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Hear me, my lord;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: What need you five and twenty, ten, or five,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: To follow in a house where twice so many

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Have a command to tend you?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: What need one?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: O, reason not the need: our basest beggars

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Are in the poorest thing superfluous:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Allow not nature more than nature needs,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Man’s life’s as cheap as beast’s: thou art a lady;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: If only to go warm were gorgeous,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why, nature needs not what thou gorgeous wear’st,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Which scarcely keeps thee warm. But, for true need—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You heavens, give me that patience, patience I need!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You see me here, you gods, a poor old man,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: As full of grief as age; wretched in both!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: If it be you that stir these daughters’ hearts

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Against their father, fool me not so much

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To bear it tamely; touch me with noble anger,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And let not women’s weapons, water-drops,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Stain my man’s cheeks! No, you unnatural hags,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I will have such revenges on you both,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That all the world shall—I will do such things—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What they are, yet I know not: but they shall be

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The terrors of the earth. You think I’ll weep

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, I’ll not weep:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I have full cause of weeping; but this heart

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Shall break into a hundred thousand flaws,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Or ere I’ll weep. O fool, I shall go mad!

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Let us withdraw; ’twill be a storm.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: This house is little: the old man and his people

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Cannot be well bestow’d.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: ’Tis his own blame; hath put himself from rest,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: And must needs taste his folly.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: For his particular, I’ll receive him gladly,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: But not one follower.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: So am I purposed.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Where is my lord of Gloucester?

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Follow’d the old man forth: he is return’d.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The king is in high rage.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Whither is he going?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He calls to horse; but will I know not whither.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: ’Tis best to give him way; he leads himself.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: My lord, entreat him by no means to stay.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Alack, the night comes on, and the bleak winds

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Do sorely ruffle; for many miles a bout

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: There’s scarce a bush.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: O, sir, to wilful men,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: The injuries that they themselves procure

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Must be their schoolmasters. Shut up your doors:

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: He is attended with a desperate train;

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: And what they may incense him to, being apt

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: To have his ear abused, wisdom bids fear.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Shut up your doors, my lord; ’tis a wild night:

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: My Regan counsels well; come out o’ the storm.

stage_directions.text: Enter King Lear, Fool, and Gentleman.

That sir which serves and seeks for gain, And follows but for form, Will pack when it begins to rain, And leave thee in the storm, But I will tarry; the fool will stay, And let the wise man fly: The knave turns fool that runs away; The fool no knave, perdy.
Enter Cornwall, Regan, Gloucester, and Servants.

If you do love old men, if your sweet sway Allow obedience, if yourselves are old, Make it your cause; send down, and take my part! To Goneril. Art not ashamed to look upon this beard? O Regan, wilt thou take her by the hand?
Exeunt King Lear, Gloucester, Kent, and Fool.

annotations.title: 'Pelican daughters' — a folk-belief turned metaphor
annotations.body: Lear calls his daughters 'pelican daughters' in a line anticipating 3.4. The pelican was believed (incorrectly, but persistently in folk tradition) to feed its young with blood pecked from its own breast. 'Pelican daughters' inverts the image: children who drain their parents' blood rather than receive it. The pelican was a medieval emblem of Christ — self-sacrificing parental love — so the inversion has theological weight. Goneril and Regan are anti-pelicans, anti-Christs in miniature. This is one of Lear's most compressed metaphors, and it matters that the Fool must speak the play's lighter truths while Lear reaches for these darker emblems.
sources: Henry Peacham, Minerva Britanna (1612) — contemporary emblem source on the pelican myth

annotations.title: 'O, reason not the need!' — Lear's arithmetic of dignity
annotations.body: Regan's question 'What need one?' — why do you need even a single attendant — strips Lear to his last arithmetic. His response, 'O, reason not the need!', is one of the most important speeches in the play. 'Our basest beggars / Are in the poorest thing superfluous; / Allow not nature more than nature needs, / Man's life is cheap as beast's.' Lear's claim: human dignity requires a margin of excess. A life calibrated to pure biological necessity is not a human life. The speech is also self-wounding — Lear has just been a king who demanded 'natural' filial love while also demanding a hundred-knight retinue; his daughters are using his logic against him. The scene ends with Lear rushing out into the storm and the famous 'Shut up your doors.' The threshold between civilization and exposure literally closes on him.
sources: Stanley Cavell, 'The Avoidance of Love' in Disowning Knowledge (1987)

annotations.title: 'Hysterica passio' — the mother rising in a male body
annotations.body: Lear names his rising grief 'Hysterica passio' — 'the mother' — and tells it to 'climb no more.' This draws on Edward Jorden's 1603 'A Briefe Discourse of a Disease called the Suffocation of the Mother,' which had recently argued (controversially) that 'hysteria' (from Greek 'hystera,' womb) was a medical condition with natural causes. Jorden's book was partly a response to the witchcraft panics — it argued that symptoms blamed on demons were actually bodily. Lear applying a feminine-coded diagnosis to his own rising feeling is strange, a king appropriating a medical language usually reserved for women. The effect doubles the play's interest in gender-destabilized grief: Lear crying 'the mother' reaches, involuntarily, for an anatomy not his.
sources: Edward Jorden, A Briefe Discourse of a Disease called the Suffocation of the Mother (1603)

options: Pelicans were believed to feed their young with blood pecked from their own breast — 'pelican daughters' inverts the image: children who drain their parents

trials.prompt: What does Lear's 'O, reason not the need!' argue?
options: That human dignity requires a margin of excess above biological necessity — a life calibrated to pure need is not a human life
That he has earned the retinue

trials.prompt: The line 'Shut up your doors' at the end of 2.4 is important because —
options: The threshold between civilization and exposure literally closes on Lear — this is the moment that sends him into the storm scenes of Act 3
It is a stage direction only
It is spoken by the Fool

trials.prompt: What is 'hysterica passio' (the mother) that Lear names rising in him?
options: A medical diagnosis from Edward Jorden's 1603 treatise — typically associated with female bodies; Lear's appropriation of it is strange and gendered
